2.15: spoudason seauton dokimon parastēsai to theo ergaten anepaiskunton orthotomounta ton logon tes aletheias (spoudason seauton dokimon parastēsai tō theō, ergatēn anepaiskunton, orthotomounta ton logon tēs alētheias): 'Do your best to present yourself to God as one approved, a worker who has no need to be ashamed, rightly handling the word of truth.' Orthotomounta (rightly dividing/handling/cutting): used in LXX Prov 3:6 ('he will make straight your paths') and 11:5. The word evokes a craftsman cutting or laying material in a straight line — a road-builder, a carpenter, a surgeon. The image: the handling of the word of truth requires the precision and skill of a craftsman who cuts straight rather than crooked. Historically, the King James 'rightly dividing' was applied dispensationally; the original metaphor is about competent, accurate, straight-course exposition.

3.16: pasa graphe theopneustos kai ophelimos (pāsa graphē theopneustos kai ōphelimos): 'All Scripture is God-breathed (theopneustos) and profitable.' Theopneustos is a Pauline coinage (hapax legomenon) combining theos (God) and pneō (breathe) — 'God-breathed' or 'breathed out by God.' The breath-metaphor echoes Gen 2:7 (YHWH breathed into Adam's nostrils) and the Spirit-wind of Ezekiel's valley of bones: the Scriptures are alive with divine breath, not merely human compositions. The primary referent is the OT (which Timothy was taught from childhood, v. 15); the claim's extension to apostolic writings is implicit in 2 Pet 3:16's treatment of Paul's letters as graphe.
